Wait, this reminds me of something called a de Bruijn sequence. Oh right! A de Bruijn sequence of order n on a k-ary alphabet contains every possible subsequence of length n exactly once. And the length of such a sequence is k^n + (n-1). So for the problem here, since we need to cover all possible passwords (which are all possible n-length sequences), generating a de Bruijn sequence would give us the minimal length string. But how to generate the de Bruijn sequence? One common method is to use a depth-first search (DFS) approach, building the sequence by traversing edges of a graph. Each node in the graph represents a (n-1)-length sequence, and each edge represents adding a new character. For example, for n=2, each node is a 1-length sequence. Each edge from node A to B represents adding a digit.
